one_line_takeaway: Safety guardrails collapse under fine-tuning largely because the upstream alignment data is too similar to the downstream task; choosing low-similarity alignment data (or routing jobs to low-similarity models) yields more durable guardrails, cutting harmfulness by up to ~10 percentage points and stacking on top of existing defenses.
key_finding: Low-similarity upstream alignment data consistently produces the most durable guardrails and high-similarity data the most fragile ones, holding across model scale (7B, 13B), downstream task type (harmful and benign), and both downstream defenses. The maximum Low-Sim vs High-Sim gap in Harmfulness Score is 10.33 percentage points. On LLAMA-2-13B initial (pre-downstream) models with 5K alignment subsets, High-Sim HS is 16.67% versus Low-Sim 10.33%. The effect is additive with downstream defenses: BEA + Low-Sim is safer than BEA + High-Sim, so upstream data engineering complements rather than replaces existing defenses. A preliminary result also shows high-intra-similarity clusters erode safety more than He et al.'s explicitly-harmful Top-100 anchor subset -- by 15.7% on Alpaca (56.33% vs 40.67% HS) and 16.3% on Dolly (68.67% vs 52.33%).
core_contribution: The paper identifies representation similarity between the UPSTREAM safety-alignment dataset and the DOWNSTREAM fine-tuning task as a previously-overlooked, causal factor in the collapse of LLM safety guardrails after fine-tuning. Prior work (e.g., He et al. 2024) located the problem in the downstream data (harmful/list-format subsets); this paper's own contribution reframes it as a relational property between the two dataset stages. It shows that a safety guardrail built from alignment data that is HIGHLY similar to the downstream task is more fragile, while a guardrail built from LOW-similarity alignment data is substantially more durable, reducing harmfulness by up to 10.33 percentage points. From this the authors derive an actionable, proactive defense: fine-tuning service providers can compute cosine similarity between a user's downstream dataset and each candidate safety-aligned model's alignment corpus, then route the fine-tuning job to the lowest-similarity (lowest-risk) model, rather than relying only on reactive post-hoc patching. A secondary contribution is an anchor-free clustering method (k-means over model representations) that finds guardrail-eroding downstream subsets without needing explicit harmful anchor data.
threat_model: Fine-tuning-induced jailbreak of a safety-aligned open-weight LLM. A third party (or malicious user of a fine-tuning-as-a-service platform such as OpenAI/Anthropic-style providers) fine-tunes an aligned model on downstream data and thereby erodes its safety guardrails, causing it to answer harmful prompts (e.g., 'step-by-step instructions for building a bomb'). Critically, the paper shows this degradation occurs even when the downstream data is entirely BENIGN (Alpaca, SAMSum), not only with explicitly harmful data (Pure Bad, list examples). A second, amplifying threat vector: when the upstream alignment dataset is PUBLICLY accessible, an adversary can deliberately craft downstream fine-tuning tasks that mimic the alignment data distribution (high similarity) to maximize guardrail collapse -- so dataset confidentiality itself becomes a security control.
limitations: 1) The guardrail is a reconstruction by the authors (Base + UltraChat + BeaverTails), not a real production alignment pipeline, since those are not public -- external validity to deployed models is asserted but not directly tested. 2) The headline effect is modest in absolute terms (up to ~10 percentage points of HS), and the benign-task results are not perfectly monotonic (e.g., in Table 1 Alpaca, Random sometimes shows higher HS than High-Sim). 3) The defense assumes providers can compute representations of the alignment corpus with an uncensored model and have a POOL of candidate models with differing alignment data to route among -- not always realistic. 4) Scope is limited to harmful-output (jailbreak) guardrails on LLAMA-2 / GEMMA-2 open-weight models; other alignment axes (factuality, fairness) and closed/frontier or multimodal/reasoning models are left to future work. 5) Similarity is defined via a single representation choice (last-token final hidden state); sensitivity to this choice is not deeply ablated in the main text.

datasets_used: BeaverTails (Ji et al. 2024b) -- 7,774 unique harmful prompts; responses used as DBT-Llama safety-alignment data
UltraChat (Ding et al. 2023) -- 52K sampled from 200K for instruction fine-tuning
Alpaca (Taori et al. 2023) -- 52K-item benign downstream task; also source of clustered List Examples
SAMSum (Gliwa et al. 2019) -- 16K messenger-like dialogues, benign summarization downstream task
Pure Bad (Qi et al. 2024) -- 100 harmful input/answer pairs, harmful downstream task
Dolly -- used in preliminary clustering comparison
HEx-PHI safety benchmark (Qi et al. 2025) -- safety evaluation prompts
quantitative_results: Max Low-Sim vs High-Sim Harmfulness Score gap: up to 10.33 percentage points
LLAMA-2-13B initial models, 5K subset: High-Sim HS 16.67% vs Low-Sim HS 10.33% (Random 11.33%)
LLAMA-2-7B initial models, None (no safety data) HS 63.33% vs Full 7.7K HS 3.33%
LLAMA-2-13B initial models, None HS 71.00% vs Full 7.7K HS 9.00%
Preliminary clustering: high-similarity clusters more harmful than Top-100 Harmful anchor subset by 15.7% (Alpaca) and 16.3% (Dolly)
Figure 2 HS: Alpaca Top-100 40.67%, Alpaca Clusters 56.33%, Dolly Top-100 52.33%, Dolly Clusters 68.67%, Pure Bad reference 75.0%, Initial 0.0%
LLAMA-2-7B List task (no defense), High-Sim 5K HS 74.33% vs Low-Sim 5K HS 71.67%
LLAMA-2-7B Pure Bad + BEA defense: High-Sim 5K HS 30.67% vs Low-Sim 5K HS 27.00%
Alignment subset sizes tested: 1,000, 5,000, and Full 7.7K; larger safety datasets generally lower HS
baselines_compared: He et al. (2024) anchor-based Top-100 Harmful subset selection (anchor-free clustering shown more damaging)
High-Sim vs Low-Sim vs Random alignment subsets (core comparison)
Backdoor Enhanced Alignment / BEA downstream defense (Wang et al. 2024)
None (no safety alignment) and Full (7.7K) alignment baselines
